355	  symmetry used in the inductive step of \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
356	\end{definition}
357	
358	The strong-monoidality comparison \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
359	below is assembled from three reduction lemmas --- a localization criterion, the
360	local-isomorphism property of the unit, and their corollary --- together with the
361	coequalizer presentation of the relative module-presheaf tensor product, the single
362	Mathlib-absent brick on which the comparison rests.
363	
364	\begin{lemma}[Localization criterion for module sheafification]
365	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
366	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
367	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
368	  For a morphism \(f : \mathcal{P} \to \mathcal{Q}\) of presheaves of
369	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the sheafification \((f)^{\#}\)
370	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules if
371	  and only if the underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves lies in the
372	  weak-equivalence class \(J.W\) of the opens topology \(J\) on \(X\) (i.e.\ is a
373	  local isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves).
374	\end{lemma}
375	
376	\begin{proof}
377	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
378	  Module sheafification (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is the localization
379	  functor at the class \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying
380	  abelian-presheaf morphism lies in \(J.W\), i.e.\ the inverse image of \(J.W\) along
381	  the forgetful functor. The localization criterion for a reflective adjunction
382	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) then gives the stated
383	  equivalence.
384	\end{proof}
385	
386	\begin{lemma}[The sheafification unit is an abelian local isomorphism]
387	  \label{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
388	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
389	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
390	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the underlying
391	  abelian-group presheaf morphism of the sheafification unit
392	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\)
393	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) is the abelian sheafification
394	  unit \(\mathcal{P} \to a(\mathcal{P})\); it therefore lies in the weak-equivalence
395	  class \(J.W\) of the opens topology.
396	\end{lemma}
397	
398	\begin{proof}
399	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
400	  The forgetful functor to abelian-group presheaves carries the module
401	  sheafification unit to the unit of the abelian associated-sheaf adjunction, which
402	  is a local isomorphism, hence a member of \(J.W\).
403	\end{proof}
404	
405	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the localization unit]
406	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
408	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
409	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the sheafification
410	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})^{\#}\) of the localization unit is an isomorphism of sheaves
411	  of modules. This is the un-whiskered (\(m = 0\)) special case of
412	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}.
413	\end{lemma}
414	
415	\begin{proof}
416	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
417	  Feed the abelian local-isomorphism statement
418	  \cref{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit} through the localization criterion
419	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}.
420	\end{proof}
421	
422	\begin{lemma}[Relative tensor of module presheaves as a coequalizer]
423	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
424	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relativeTensorCoequalizerIso}
425	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
426	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
427	  underlying abelian-group presheaf of their relative tensor product
428	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
429	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is naturally the coequalizer of the
430	  parallel pair
431	  \[
432	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
433	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
434	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
435	  \]
436	  of abelian-group presheaves, where the first map acts the ring factor on the left,
437	  \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto (r \cdot p) \otimes q\), and the second on the
438	  right, \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto p \otimes (r \cdot q)\). The identification
439	  is objectwise and natural in \(U\).
440	\end{lemma}
441	
442	\begin{proof}
443	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
444	  Over each open \(U\) the module
445	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is, by the very
446	  definition of the relative tensor product, the quotient of the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor
447	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) by the subgroup generated by
448	  the elements \((r \cdot p) \otimes q - p \otimes (r \cdot q)\); equivalently it is
449	  the coequalizer of the two \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear action maps displayed above. Since
450	  colimits of abelian-group presheaves are computed objectwise, these objectwise
451	  coequalizers assemble into a coequalizer in
452	  \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\); naturality in \(U\) is the
453	  compatibility of the action maps with the restriction maps of
454	  \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{Q}\).
455	\end{proof}
456	
457	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
458	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
459	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
460	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
461	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
462	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
463	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
464	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
465	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
466	  the sheafification adjunction
467	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
468	  presheaves of modules, and write
469	  \[
470	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
471	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
472	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
473	  \]
474	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
475	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
476	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
477	  \[
478	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
479	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
480	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
481	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
482	  \]
483	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
484	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
485	\end{lemma}
486	
487	\begin{proof}
488	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
489	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
490	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
491	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
492	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
493	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
494	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
495	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
496	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
497	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
498	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
499	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
500	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
501	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
502	
503	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
504	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
505	  is the objectwise relative tensor
506	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
507	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
508	  maps
509	  \[
510	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
511	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
512	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
513	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
514	  \]
515	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
516	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
517	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
518	  pair
519	  \[
520	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
521	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
522	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
523	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
524	  \]
525	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
526	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
527	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
528	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
529	
530	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
531	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
532	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
533	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
534	  of \(a\)-images. Now the localization unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}}\) lies in
535	  \(J.W\), and the class \(J.W\) is \emph{monoidal} for the
536	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor: whiskering a local isomorphism by
537	  \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) again yields a local isomorphism. (This is the
538	  abelian monoidality of \(J.W\), itself proved through the fact that the
539	  internal hom into a sheaf is again a sheaf.) Consequently the two whiskered
540	  comparison maps
541	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) and
542	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
543	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) relating the two rows are
544	  isomorphisms.
545	
546	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
547	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
548	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
549	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
550	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
551	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
552	\end{proof}
553	
554	\begin{corollary}[Sheaf-level associator on tensor products]
555	  \label{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
556	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjAssoc}
557	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
558	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
559	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
560	  For sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B},
561	  \mathcal{C}\) there is a canonical associativity isomorphism
562	  \[
563	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} :
564	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{B})
565	      \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C}
566	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
567	    \mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
568	      (\mathcal{B} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C})
569	  \]
570	  of sheaves of modules (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), natural in all three
571	  arguments.
572	\end{corollary}
573	
574	\begin{proof}
575	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
576	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
577	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
578	  Write \(a, b, c\) for the underlying presheaves of modules of
579	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) and \((-)^{\#}\) for sheafification
580	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}). By
581	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} the two iterated products unfold to
582	  \[
583	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
584	      = \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#},
585	    \qquad
586	    \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
587	      = \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#},
588	  \]
589	  each carrying an \emph{inner} sheafification that obstructs a direct appeal to
590	  the presheaf associator. The whiskered units remove these inner
591	  sheafifications. Applying \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
592	  with \(\mathcal{P} = a \otimes_p b\) and \(\mathcal{Q} = c\), the morphism
593	  \[
594	    (\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#} :
595	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
596	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
597	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
598	    = (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
599	  \]
600	  is an isomorphism; and, reading the same lemma through the braiding of the
601	  presheaf monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) so as
602	  to whisker the unit \(\eta_{b \otimes_p c}\) on the left of \(a\), the
603	  morphism
604	  \[
605	    (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#} :
606	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)\bigr)^{\#}
607	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
608	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#}
609	    = \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
610	  \]
611	  is an isomorphism. Between the two un-sheafified middle terms sits the
612	  sheafification of the presheaf associator
613	  \[
614	    \alpha^p_{a,b,c} :
615	    (a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c
616	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
617	    a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)
618	  \]
619	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), an isomorphism because
620	  sheafification is a functor and \(\alpha^p\) is one. The associator is the
621	  composite
622	  \[
623	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}}
624	    = (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#}
625	      \circ (\alpha^p_{a,b,c})^{\#}
626	      \circ \bigl((\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#}\bigr)^{-1},
627	  \]
628	  an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
629	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) follows from naturality of the unit,
630	  of the whiskerings, and of the presheaf associator.
631	\end{proof}
632	
633	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
634	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
635	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
636	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
637	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
638	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
639	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
640	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
641	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
642	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
643	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
644	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
645	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
646	  %  (formulation omitted)."
647	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
648	  power isomorphisms).}
649	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
650	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
651	  \[
652	    \mu_{m,m'} :
653	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
654	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
655	  \]
656	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
657	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
658	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
659	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
660	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
661	\end{lemma}
662	
663	\begin{proof}
664	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
665	    def:tensorObjUnitIso, def:tensorBraiding,
666	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
667	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
668	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
669	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
670	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
671	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
672	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
673	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
674	
675	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
676	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
677	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
678	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
679	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
680	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
681	  sheaf tensor products
682	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
683	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
684	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
685	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
686	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
687	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
688	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
689	  of the tensor product is never used.
690	
691	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
692	  \(m = 0\),
693	  \[
694	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
695	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
696	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
697	  \]
698	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
699	  recursive definition
700	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
701	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
702	  \[
703	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
704	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
705	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
706	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
707	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
708	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
709	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
710	  \]
711	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
712	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
713	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
714	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
715	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
716	  \[
717	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
718	      \mathcal{L}
719	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
720	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
721	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
722	  \]
723	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
724	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
725	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
726	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
727	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
728	
729	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
730	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
731	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
732	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
733	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
734	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
735	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
736	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
737	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
738	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
739	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
740	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
741	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
742	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
743	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
744	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
745	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
746	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
747	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
748	\end{proof}
749	
750	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
751	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
752	
753	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
754	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
755	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
756	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
757	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
758	global sections.
759	
760	\begin{definition}\leanok
761	[Section multiplication]
762	  \label{def:sectionMul}
763	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
764	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
765	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
766	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
767	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
768	  \[
769	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
770	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
771	    \;\longrightarrow\;
772	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
773	  \]
774	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
775	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
776	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
777	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
778	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
779	  the sheafification unit
780	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
781	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
782	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
783	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
784	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
785	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
786	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
787	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
788	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
789	\end{definition}
790	
791	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
792	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
793	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
794	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
795	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
796	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
797	  the two composites
798	  \[
799	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
800	    \longrightarrow
801	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
802	    \cong
803	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
804	  \]
805	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
806	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
807	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
808	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
809	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
810	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
811	\end{lemma}
812	
813	\begin{proof}
814	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
815	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
816	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
817	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
818	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
819	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
820	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
821	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
822	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
823	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
824	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
825	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
826	  section multiplications.
827	\end{proof}
828	
829	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
830	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
831	
832	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
833	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
834	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
835	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
836	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
837	
838	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
839	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
840	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
841	  \mathlibok
842	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
843	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
844	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
845	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
846	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
847	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
848	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
849	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
850	  \(1 \in A_0\).
851	\end{lemma}
852	
853	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
854	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
855	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
856	  \mathlibok
857	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
858	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
859	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
860	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
861	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
862	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
863	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
864	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
865	  extension of the graded action.
866	\end{lemma}
867	
868	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
869	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
870	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
871	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
872	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
873	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
874	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
875	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
876	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
878	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
879	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
880	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
881	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
882	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
883	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
884	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
885	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
886	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
887	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
888	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
889	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
890	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
891	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
892	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
893	  multiplication is the composite
894	  \[
895	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
896	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
897	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
898	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
899	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
900	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
901	  \]
902	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
903	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
904	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
905	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
906	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
907	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
908	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
909	\end{lemma}
910	
911	\begin{proof}
912	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
913	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
914	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
915	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
916	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of
917	  the comparison isomorphisms \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}); graded
918	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined
919	  with the commutativity constraint of \(\mu\); two-sided unitality is the unit
920	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with
921	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\). These are exactly the axioms of a
922	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\), so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
923	  endows \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its
924	  commutative-semiring structure.
925	\end{proof}
926	
927	\begin{lemma}[Graded module structure on the twisted-section components]
928	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
929	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
930	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
931	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib,
932	    lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
933	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
934	  % `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
935	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4279-L4286).
936	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
937	  % "Given a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ we set
938	  %  $$
939	  %  \Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L}, \mathcal{F})
940	  %  =
941	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{n \in \mathbf{Z}} \Gamma(X,
942	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})
943	  %  $$
944	  %  which we think of as a graded $\Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L})$-module."
945	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
946	  module \(\Gamma_*(X,\mathcal{L},\mathcal{F})\)).}
947	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf and \(L_s\) a line bundle on
948	  \(X_s\). The family
949	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
950	  (\cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}) carries a graded module structure (a
951	  \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}) over the graded
952	  semiring of \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}, with degree-\((m,m')\)
953	  action
954	  \[
955	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
956	      \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
957	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
958	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes
959	      (\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})\bigr)
960	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
961	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
962	  \]
963	  the section multiplication followed by the comparison isomorphism that
964	  reassociates and merges the line-bundle factors (using
965	  \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add} and the symmetry of the tensor product).
966	  Consequently \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
967	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a graded module over
968	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\); this is the graded-module structure underlying
969	  \cref{def:sectionGradedModule}.
970	\end{lemma}
971	
972	\begin{proof}
973	  \uses{lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:sheafTensorPow_add,
974	    lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib, lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
975	  The action maps are the displayed composites. The graded-module axioms ---
976	  compatibility of the action with the ring multiplication
977	  (\(r \cdot (r' \cdot x) = (r r') \cdot x\)) and unitality
978	  (\(1 \cdot x = x\)) --- follow from the associativity and unit coherences of
979	  the section multiplication (\cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent}) together with the
980	  associativity and commutativity constraints of the comparison isomorphisms
981	  (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), exactly as in the proof of
982	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring} but with the third tensor factor
983	  carrying \(\mathcal{F}_s\). These are the axioms of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), so
984	  \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib} produces the module structure of
985	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over \(R(X_s, L_s)\).
986	\end{proof}

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

909	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
910	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
911	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
912	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
913	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
914	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
915	
916	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
917	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
918	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
919	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
920	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
921	  \(A_g\).
922	
923	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
924	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
925	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
926	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
927	
928	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
929	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
930	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
931	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
932	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
933	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
934	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
935	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
936	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
937	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
938	
939	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
940	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
941	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
942	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
943	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
944	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
945	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
946	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
947	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
948	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
949	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
950	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
951	
952	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
953	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
954	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
955	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
956	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
957	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
958	
959	  \emph{Action-diamond subtlety.} The conclusion of the lemma requires an
960	  \(A_g\)-module structure on \(T_g\) sitting in a scalar tower
961	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). One cannot simply install an anonymous
962	  \(A_g\)-action by fiat: \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already carries its \emph{canonical}
963	  localisation action of \(A_g\), and this canonical action is the one the scalar-tower
964	  requirement sees. Hence the identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must be promoted to an
965	  \(A_g\)-linear isomorphism — which it is, because the transported action agrees with the
966	  canonical localisation action on the \(A_g\)-constants: the constant
967	  \(\mathcal{C}\)-action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors through the original \(A\)-action on
968	  \(T\) exactly as the canonical \(g\)-localisation action does. With the isomorphism made
969	  \(A_g\)-linear, the \(R\)-module structure and finiteness pull back from \(T'_g\) to
970	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
971	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.
972	
973	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
974	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
975	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
976	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
977	\end{proof}
978	
979	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
980	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
981	
982	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
983	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
984	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
985	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
986	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
987	
988	\begin{lemma}\leanok
989	[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
990	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
991	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
992	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
993	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
994	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
995	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
996	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
997	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
998	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
999	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1000	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1001	\end{lemma}
1002	\begin{proof}
1003	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1004	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1005	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1006	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1007	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1008	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1009	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1010	\end{proof}
1011	
1012	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1013	[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1014	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1015	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1016	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1017	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1018	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1019	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1020	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1021	\end{lemma}
1022	\begin{proof}
1023	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1024	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1025	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1026	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1027	\end{proof}
1028	
1029	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1030	[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1031	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1032	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1033	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1034	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1035	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1036	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1037	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1038	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1039	\end{lemma}
1040	\begin{proof}
1041	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1042	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1043	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1044	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1045	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1046	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1047	  these conditions gives the claim.
1048	\end{proof}
1049	
1050	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1051	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}
1052	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated}
1053	  \mathlibok
1054	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1055	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1056	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1057	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1058	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1059	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).
1060	\end{lemma}

1062	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1063	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1064	  \lean{Module.Free.of_ringEquiv}
1065	  \mathlibok
1066	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1067	  Let \(R, R'\) be rings, \(M\) an \(R\)-module and \(M'\) an \(R'\)-module, and let
1068	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) be an additive isomorphism that is semilinear over a ring
1069	  isomorphism \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\), i.e.\ \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\).
1070	  If \(M\) is free over \(R\), then \(M'\) is free over \(R'\).
1071	\end{lemma}
1072	
1073	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1074	[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1075	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1076	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1077	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1078	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1079	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1080	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1081	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1082	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1083	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1084	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1085	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1086	\end{lemma}
1087	
1088	\begin{proof}
1089	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1090	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1091	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1092	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1093	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1094	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1095	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1096	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1097	
1098	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1099	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1100	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1101	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1102	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1103	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1104	
1105	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1106	  \[
1107	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1108	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1109	  \]
1110	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1111	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1112	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1113	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1114	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1115	
1116	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1117	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1118	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1119	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1120	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1121	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1122	\end{proof}
1123	
1124	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1125	[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1126	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
1127	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
1128	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1129	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1130	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1131	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1132	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
1133	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1134	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
1135	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
1136	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1137	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1138	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1139	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1140	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1141	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1142	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1143	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1144	  core of the induction).}
1145	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1146	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1147	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1148	
1149	\end{lemma}
1150	
1151	\begin{proof}
1152	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1153	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1154	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1155	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1156	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1157	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1158	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1159	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1160	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1161	
1162	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1163	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1164	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1165	
1166	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1167	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1168	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1169	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1170	  \begin{enumerate}
1171	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1172	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1173	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1174	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1175	      \(P_d\)-modules
1176	      \[
1177	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1178	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1179	      \]
1180	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1181	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1182	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1183	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1184	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1185	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1186	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1187	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1188	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1189	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1190	      to \(A_g\).
1191	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1192	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1193	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1194	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1195	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1196	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1197	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1198	      over \(A_f\).
1199	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1200	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1201	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1202	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1203	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1204	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1205	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1206	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1207	      completes the induction.
1208	  \end{enumerate}
1209	\end{proof}

1255	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1256	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1257	
1258	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1259	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1260	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1261	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1262	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1263	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1264	restricted sheaf over the base open.
1265	
1266	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness is local on a finite spanning family]
1267	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1268	  \lean{Module.Finite.of_localizationSpan_finite}
1269	  \mathlibok
1270	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalProperties.Submodule}).}
1271	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(N\) an \(R\)-module, and \(t \subseteq R\) a
1272	  finite family with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = \top\) (the \(t\) generate the unit
1273	  ideal). Suppose that for each \(g \in t\) the localization \(N_g\) is a finite
1274	  module over the localization \(R_g\). Then \(N\) is a finite \(R\)-module.
1275	\end{lemma}

1277	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1278	[Half 1 helper — transfer of localized-module finiteness across models]
1279	  \label{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer}
1280	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.finite_localizedModule_of_isLocalizedModule}
1281	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative submonoid,
1282	  and \(M\) an \(R\)-module. Let \(R_S\) be an \(R\)-algebra that localizes \(R\)
1283	  at \(S\), and let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module carrying a compatible \(R_S\)-module
1284	  structure, together with an \(R\)-linear map \(\varphi : M \to N\) exhibiting
1285	  \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\) (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,
1286	  \varphi\)). If \(N\) is a finite \(R_S\)-module, then the canonical localized
1287	  module \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) is a finite module over the canonical
1288	  localization \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1289	\end{lemma}
1290	
1291	\begin{proof}
1292	  The localization map \(\varphi\) provides an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
1293	  \(e : N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) (universal property
1294	  of localized modules), and the algebra localization provides an \(R\)-algebra
1295	  isomorphism \(\psi : R_S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Localization}\,S\). These
1296	  are compatible: \(e\) is semilinear over \(\psi\), i.e.\ \(e(a \cdot x) =
1297	  \psi(a) \cdot e(x)\) for \(a \in R_S\), \(x \in N\) (both sides are pinned by
1298	  clearing a common denominator \(s \in S\), where the \(R\)-actions agree). A
1299	  finite \(R_S\)-spanning set \(T\) of \(N\) therefore maps under \(e\) to a
1300	  finite set whose \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\)-span is all of
1301	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\); hence the canonical model is finite over
1302	  \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1303	\end{proof}

1305	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1306	[Half 1 — G1 locality reduction over a finite basic-open cover]
1307	  \label{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1308	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1309	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,

1311	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof, gluing step
1312	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2107-L2109).
1313	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1314	  % "Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$. Hence we conclude by
1315	  %  Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover}."
1316	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (gluing step,
1317	  Algebra Lemma \texttt{lemma-cover}).}
1318	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(X\), and
1319	  \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Suppose \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) is a finite
1320	  family with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) (so the basic opens \(D(g)\),
1321	  \(g \in t\), cover \(W\)), and that for each \(g \in t\) the section module
1322	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). Then \(\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1323	  is a finite \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
1324	\end{lemma}
1325	
1326	\begin{proof}
1327	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1328	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1329	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (gluing step).}
1330	  By the local-on-a-spanning-family criterion \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1331	  it suffices to show, for each \(g \in t\), that the localization of
1332	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\) is finite over the localization
1333	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). Fix \(g \in t\). The basic open \(D(g)\) is affine, and the
1334	  restriction \(\Gamma(X, W) \to \Gamma(X, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\) as
1335	  the away-localization \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1336	  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); moreover the section restriction
1337	  \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) as the
1338	  localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1339	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). Thus \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is a
1340	  model of the localized module, finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = (\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1341	  by hypothesis; the model-transfer lemma
1342	  \cref{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer} carries this finiteness to the
1343	  canonical localized module of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at the powers of \(g\), over
1344	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). This supplies the per-element hypothesis, and
1345	  \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan} concludes that \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is
1346	  finite over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
